nativeRange.habitat: Rocky reefs in the Eastern Pacific, typically at depths of 6 to 12 metres. Adults are non-migratory and tend to remain within a defined home range.

diet.notes: In the wild, the Cortez Angelfish feeds primarily on sponges, with the diet supplemented by tunicates, algae, bryozoans, hydroids, and eggs. In captivity, a varied diet of Spirulina, marine algae, mysis shrimp and other meaty foods, and high-quality angelfish preparations that contain sponge matter is generally recommended. Feeding several small meals throughout the day tends to produce better results than one or two large feedings.

imageCandidates.notes: Alberto Alcala; 2048x1536; sharp in-situ adult clearly showing blue-grey speckled body, vivid yellow shoulder stripe and chest, and blue-lipped dark head - all key adult diagnostics.
Alberto Alcala; 2048x1564; whole adult against clean open blue water, sharp, showing yellow shoulder bar and speckled midsection from a second angle.
LASZLO ILYES; 3325x2217; crisp, vibrant juvenile showing the dramatic juvenile phase - dark body with bright yellow and electric-blue curved vertical bands.

summary: The Cortez Angelfish is a large Eastern Pacific species reaching up to 46 cm, notable for a dramatic color change: juveniles display bright yellow and electric-blue vertical bands, while adults develop a blue body with pale speckling and vertical yellow shoulder stripes. Semi-aggressive and not considered reef safe.
careNotes: The Cortez Angelfish (Pomacanthus zonipectus) is a large Eastern Pacific angelfish native to the Gulf of California and ranging south to Peru, where it occupies rocky reefs at moderate depths. Adults typically reach about 26 cm but can grow to a maximum of around 46 cm, so a long, well-aquascaped aquarium of at least 1000 litres with plentiful live rock for grazing and refuge is generally advised. Juveniles are dark with bright yellow and electric-blue vertical bands, while adults develop a blue body with a pale speckled mid-section, vertical yellow shoulder stripes, green-blue dorsal fins, and a blue head — the two colour phases look so different that juveniles are sometimes mistaken for a separate species. The Cortez Angelfish is not considered reef safe: it has been documented eating sponges, both stony and soft corals, and clam mantles. Only one angelfish of any species should be kept per aquarium, even at juvenile size, due to the high aggression this species tends to show toward conspecifics. In the wild, adults are generally observed in monogamous pairs or loose aggregations and breed midsummer through early fall. As a large Pomacanthus, it is reportedly susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition in which pitting and discolouration develop along the head and sensory canals — when kept in poor water quality or fed a sponge-deficient diet, so vitamin-enriched marine algae and sponge-containing foods are commonly recommended.
breedingNotes: In the wild, adults form monogamous pairs and spawn pelagically (releasing eggs and sperm into open water) between midsummer and early fall. Captive breeding has not been documented for this species, and the Cortez Angelfish is not considered a viable candidate for aquarium propagation.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacanthidae / Acanthuriformes), maximum size (46 cm TL vs LiveAquaria's 18 in / 45.7 cm — agreement), wild temperature range (20.4–29.1°C vs LiveAquaria's 22.2–25.6°C — minor disagreement, written range reflects intermediate aquarium target), native range (Eastern Pacific from Gulf of California to Peru), depth (6–12 m), diet (sponge-dominant), monogamous pair behavior, and breeding seasonality.

fish.breedingNotes: In the wild, the Cortez Angelfish breeds as a monogamous pair, releasing eggs and sperm into open water between midsummer and early fall. It has not been meaningfully bred in captivity.
